title.raw: 卷六十 杜周傳 Volume 60: Du Zhou

sentences.zh: 杜周，南陽杜衍人也。

translations.literal: Du Zhou was a man of Duyang in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Du Zhou came from Duyang in Nanyang commandery.

sentences.zh: 義縱為南陽太守，以周為爪牙，薦之張湯，為廷尉史。

translations.literal: Yi Zong was governor of Nanyang; he took Zhou as claw and fang, recommended him to Zhang Tang, and he became a clerk of the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: When Yi Zong served as governor of Nanyang, he used Du Zhou as his enforcer and recommended him to Zhang Tang, who took him on as a clerk in the office of the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 使案邊失亡，所論殺甚多。

translations.literal: He was sent to investigate border losses and desertions; those he sentenced and killed were very many.
translations.idiomatic: He was dispatched to investigate frontier casualties and desertions; the death sentences he procured ran very high.

sentences.zh: 奏事中意，任用，與減宣更為中丞者十餘歲。

translations.literal: His memorials hit the ruler's intent; he was employed, and with Jian Xuan alternated as vice director of the secretariat for more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: His reports always matched what the emperor wanted to hear, and he rose steadily, serving for over ten years as vice director of the secretariat in rotation with Jian Xuan.

sentences.zh: 周少言重遲，而內深次骨。

translations.literal: Zhou spoke little, was heavy and slow, yet inwardly was deep to the marrow.
translations.idiomatic: Du Zhou was a man of few words, slow and ponderous in manner, but his cruelty ran marrow-deep.

sentences.zh: 宣為左內史，周為廷尉，其治大抵放張湯，而善候司。

translations.literal: When Xuan was left metropolitan superintendent, Zhou was commandant of justice; his governance largely modeled on Zhang Tang, yet he was skilled at watching and spying.
translations.idiomatic: When Jian Xuan held the left metropolitan superintendency, Du Zhou became commandant of justice. His judicial style broadly followed Zhang Tang's, but he was even better at reading the court and shadowing suspects.

sentences.zh: 上所欲擠者，因而陷之；

translations.literal: Those the ruler wished to squeeze, he thereby trapped them;
translations.idiomatic: Anyone the emperor wanted ruined, Zhou would find a way to ensnare;

sentences.zh: 上所欲釋，久系待問而微見其冤狀。

translations.literal: those the ruler wished to release, he long imprisoned awaiting interrogation while subtly revealing their wrongful condition.
translations.idiomatic: anyone the emperor wished spared he would leave in custody, dragging out the hearings until hints of their innocence could surface.

sentences.zh: 客有謂周曰：「君為天下決平，不循三尺法，專以人主意指為獄，獄者固如是乎？」

translations.literal: A guest said to Zhou, "You, sir, decide fairness for the empire; you do not follow the three-foot code, but solely take the ruler's mind-intent as prison—are prisons indeed thus?"
translations.idiomatic: A visitor once asked him, "You hold the scales of justice for the whole realm, yet you ignore the statute bamboo in favor of the emperor's whim—is that what law is meant to be?"

sentences.zh: 周曰：「三尺安出哉？

translations.literal: Zhou said, "Where does the three-foot come from?
translations.idiomatic: Du Zhou replied, "Where does the written code itself come from?

sentences.zh: 前主所是著為律，後主所是疏為令；

translations.literal: What the former ruler approved was written as statute; what the later ruler approved was spread as ordinance;
translations.idiomatic: Whatever a past sovereign endorsed became statute; whatever the present sovereign favors becomes binding policy;

sentences.zh: 當時為是，何古之法乎！」

translations.literal: what was right for the time was right—what law of antiquity!"
translations.idiomatic: the law is simply what fits the moment—why invoke some antique rulebook?"

sentences.zh: 至周為廷尉，詔獄亦益多矣。

translations.literal: When Zhou became commandant of justice, imperial-order prisons also increased.
translations.idiomatic: Once Du Zhou took the commandancy, the number of cases tried on imperial warrant multiplied.

sentences.zh: 二千石系者新故相因，不減百餘人。

translations.literal: Two-thousand-dan men imprisoned, new and old continuous, not less than a hundred persons.
translations.idiomatic: At any time no fewer than a hundred senior officials ranked at two thousand piculs languished in his jails, old cases piled atop new.

sentences.zh: 郡吏大府舉之延尉，一歲至千餘章。

translations.literal: Commandery clerks and great office recommended them to the commandant of justice; in one year it reached more than a thousand memorials.
translations.idiomatic: County yamens and capital agencies forwarded charges to his office at the rate of more than a thousand memorials a year.

sentences.zh: 章大者連逮證案數百，小者數十人；

translations.literal: Large memorials linked and arrested witnesses and cases in the hundreds; small ones several tens of persons;
translations.idiomatic: A major indictment might rope in hundreds of witnesses and co-defendants; a minor one still ensnared dozens;

sentences.zh: 遠者數千里，近者數百里。

translations.literal: the far several thousand li, the near several hundred li.
translations.idiomatic: and the accused might be dragged from thousands of li away or merely from the next province.

sentences.zh: 會獄，吏因責如章告劾，不服，以掠笞定之。

translations.literal: When cases converged in prison, clerks accordingly demanded compliance with the memorial's accusation; if they did not submit, with flogging and beating they fixed it.
translations.idiomatic: When a case came to trial, his clerks beat prisoners until they confessed to whatever the original memorial alleged.

sentences.zh: 於是聞有逮證，皆亡匿。

translations.literal: Thereupon hearing there was arrest of witnesses, all fled and hid.
translations.idiomatic: Word that one had been named a witness was enough to send men into hiding.

sentences.zh: 獄久者至更數赦十餘歲而相告言，大氐盡詆以不道，以上延尉及中都官，詔獄逮至六七萬人，吏所增加十有餘萬。

translations.literal: Those long in prison sometimes reached passing several amnesties over ten years and still mutually accusing; greatly all were slandered as "non-dao"; upward to the commandant of justice and capital offices, imperial-order prisons arrested reaching sixty or seventy thousand persons; what clerks added was more than a hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Some inmates rotted there through a dozen general amnesties, denouncing one another until nearly every charge was inflated to "immoral conduct." Cases escalated to the commandant of justice and the capital bureaus until imperial prisons held sixty or seventy thousand souls, and the clerks' own additions swelled the rolls past a hundred thousand.

sentences.zh: 周中廢，後為執金吾，逐捕桑弘羊、衛皇后昆弟子刻深，上以為盡力無私，遷為御史大夫。

translations.literal: Zhou was midway dismissed, afterward was chief of the metropolitan police, pursued and captured Sang Hongyang and Empress Wei's elder and younger brothers' sons with harsh depth; the ruler thought him exhaustive in effort without private bias, and promoted him to imperial counselor.
translations.idiomatic: Du Zhou was briefly stripped of rank, then appointed chief of police for the capital. He hunted down Sang Hongyang and the sons of Empress Wei's brothers with pitiless zeal. The emperor judged him tireless and impartial and raised him to imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 始周為廷史，有一馬，及久任事，列三公，而兩子夾河為郡守，家訾累巨萬矣。

translations.literal: At first when Zhou was a commandant clerk, he had one horse; when he long held office, ranked among the three dukes, and his two sons flanked the River as commandery governors, household property accumulated to tens of thousands of myriads.
translations.idiomatic: He had begun as a clerk with a single horse to his name; by the time he sat among the three dukes, with a son governing on each bank of the Yellow River, his family fortune ran to hundreds of millions of cash.

sentences.zh: 治皆酷暴，唯少子延年行寬厚雲。

translations.literal: Their governance was all cruel and violent; only the youngest son Yannian practiced leniency and generosity, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Both elder sons ruled with the same savagery as their father; only the youngest, Yannian, was remembered for a milder touch.

sentences.zh: 子 延年

translations.literal: Son: Yannian.
translations.idiomatic: His son: Du Yannian.

sentences.zh: 延年字幼公，亦明法律。

translations.literal: Yannian, courtesy Yougong, also understood statutes and laws.
translations.idiomatic: Du Yannian, courtesy Yougong, was likewise expert in the code.

sentences.zh: 昭帝初立，大將軍霍光秉政，以延年三公子，吏材有餘，補軍司空。

translations.literal: When Emperor Zhao first was established, Grand General Huo Guang held government; because Yannian was a son of the three dukes and had surplus clerkly talent, he supplemented as army superintendent of works.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Zhao first came to the throne, Grand General Huo Guang dominated the government. Yannian, as a son of the highest ministers and a capable administrator, was appointed superintendent of works on the general's staff.

sentences.zh: 始元四年，益州蠻夷反，延年以校尉將南陽士擊益州，還，為諫大夫。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Shiyuan, Yizhou barbarians and Yi rebelled; Yannian as colonel led Nanyang troops to strike Yizhou; returning, he became remonstrance grandee.
translations.idiomatic: In Shiyuan 4 (83 BCE) the tribes of Yizhou rose in revolt. Yannian led Nanyang militia south as a colonel, and on his return was promoted to remonstrance grandee.

sentences.zh: 左將軍上官桀父子與蓋主、燕王謀為逆亂。

translations.literal: The left general Shangguan Jie father and son with the Elder Princess of Gai and the King of Yan plotted rebellion and disorder.
translations.idiomatic: Left General Shangguan Jie and his son conspired with the Princess of Gai and the king of Yan to overturn the government.

sentences.zh: 假稻田使者燕倉知其謀，以告大司農楊敞。

translations.literal: Acting field-intendant Yan Cang knew their plot and reported it to Grand Minister of Agriculture Yang Chang.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Cang, the acting grain-field intendant, learned of the plot and carried the news to Grand Minister of Agriculture Yang Chang.

sentences.zh: 敝惶懼，移病，以語延年。

translations.literal: Chang was frightened and afraid, shifted illness, and told Yannian.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Chang panicked, pleaded illness, and confided in Yannian.

sentences.zh: 延年以聞，桀等伏辜。

translations.literal: Yannian reported it to the throne; Jie and others submitted to punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian laid the matter before the throne, and the conspirators were executed.

sentences.zh: 延年封為建平侯。

translations.literal: Yannian was enfeoffed as marquis of Jianping.
translations.idiomatic: For this service Yannian was enfeoffed as marquis of Jianping.

sentences.zh: 延年本大將軍霍光吏，首發大奸，有忠節，由是擢為太僕、右曹、給事中。

translations.literal: Yannian had originally been Grand General Huo Guang's clerk; he was first to expose great wickedness and had loyal integrity; from this he was promoted to chamberlain for the imperial stud, right bureau, and palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian had begun as a clerk on Huo Guang's staff; he was the first to expose a major treason and proved utterly loyal, and on that basis rose to chamberlain for the imperial stud, supervisor of the right bureau, and palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 光持刑罰嚴，延年輔之以寬。

translations.literal: Guang held punishments strict; Yannian assisted him with leniency.
translations.idiomatic: Where Huo Guang favored harsh sentences, Yannian softened the edge.

sentences.zh: 治燕王獄時，御史大夫桑弘羊子遷亡，過父故吏侯史吳。

translations.literal: When handling the King of Yan case, Imperial Counselor Sang Hongyang's son Qian fled, passing his father's old clerk Houshi Wu.
translations.idiomatic: During the trial of the King of Yan's revolt, Sang Hongyang's son Sang Qian fled the capital and took refuge with his father's former subordinate Houshi Wu.

sentences.zh: 後遷捕得，伏法。

translations.literal: Afterward Qian was captured and submitted to the law.
translations.idiomatic: Qian was eventually arrested and executed.

sentences.zh: 會赦，侯史吳自出系獄，廷尉王平與少府徐仁雜治反事，皆以為桑遷坐父謀反而侯史吳臧之，非匿反者，乃匿為隨者也。

translations.literal: It happened that there was an amnesty; Houshi Wu of himself came out of imprisonment; Commandant of Justice Wang Ping and chamberlain for the palace revenues Xu Ren jointly handled the rebellion case, both holding that Sang Qian sat guilty for his father's plotting rebellion while Houshi Wu harbored him—not hiding a rebel, but hiding one who had followed.
translations.idiomatic: A general amnesty followed. Houshi Wu surrendered himself. Commandant of Justice Wang Ping and Chamberlain Xu Ren jointly reviewed the treason charges. Both held that while Sang Qian was liable as his father's accomplice, Wu had merely sheltered a follower, not a principal rebel.

sentences.zh: 即以赦令除吳罪。

translations.literal: Thereupon by amnesty order they removed Wu's crime.
translations.idiomatic: They therefore struck Wu's name from the indictment under the amnesty.

sentences.zh: 後侍御史治實，以桑遷通經術，知父謀反而不諫爭，與反者身無異；

translations.literal: Afterward investigating censors handled the substance, holding that Sang Qian passed through the classics and arts, knew his father plotted rebellion yet did not remonstrate and contend—no different from the rebel in person;
translations.idiomatic: Later, investigating censors reopened the file. They argued that Sang Qian, being classically trained, must have known of his father's plot yet never spoke up—making him morally the same as a traitor;

sentences.zh: 侯史吳故三百石吏，首匿遷，不與庶人匿隨從者等，吳不得赦。

translations.literal: Houshi Wu was formerly a three-hundred-dan clerk; first to hide Qian—not equal to a commoner hiding a follower—Wu could not obtain amnesty.
translations.idiomatic: while Houshi Wu, as a former official of three hundred piculs who had knowingly concealed Qian, could not be classed with a commoner sheltering a mere retainer and therefore did not merit the amnesty.

sentences.zh: 奏請復治，劾廷尉、少府縱反者。

translations.literal: They memorialized requesting again to handle it, impeaching the commandant of justice and chamberlain for releasing rebels.
translations.idiomatic: They memorialized for a retrial and impeached Wang Ping and Xu Ren for letting traitors go free.

sentences.zh: 少府徐仁即丞相車千秋女婿也，故千秋數為侯史吳言。

translations.literal: Chamberlain Xu Ren was the son-in-law of Chancellor Che Qianqiu; therefore Qianqiu several times spoke for Houshi Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Ren was the son-in-law of Chancellor Che Qianqiu, who repeatedly interceded for Houshi Wu.

sentences.zh: 恐光不聽，千秋即召中二千石、博士會公車門，議問吳法。

translations.literal: Fearing Guang would not listen, Qianqiu thereupon summoned those of full two thousand piculs and erudits to meet at the public carriage gate, deliberating to ask Wu's law.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing Huo Guang would not relent, Che Qianqiu called a conference at the public carriage gate of every official from two thousand piculs down and the court erudits to debate how Wu should be charged.

sentences.zh: 議者知大將軍指，皆執吳為不道。

translations.literal: Those who deliberated knew the grand general's intent; all held Wu as "non-dao."
translations.idiomatic: The participants knew which way Huo Guang leaned; one after another they condemned Wu for immoral conduct.

sentences.zh: 明日，千秋封上眾議，光於是以千秋擅召中二千石以下，外內異言，遂下延尉平、少府仁獄。

translations.literal: The next day Qianqiu sealed and submitted the multitude's deliberation; Guang therefore because Qianqiu on his own summoned those below full two thousand piculs, inner and outer divergent words, sent down Commandant of Justice Ping and Chamberlain Ren to prison.
translations.idiomatic: The next day the chancellor forwarded their unanimous opinion. Huo Guang, furious that Che Qianqiu had convened a quasi-judicial assembly without authorization, had Wang Ping and Xu Ren thrown into prison.

sentences.zh: 朝廷皆恐丞相坐之。

translations.literal: The court all feared the chancellor would sit guilty.
translations.idiomatic: The whole court feared the chancellor would be dragged in next.

sentences.zh: 延年乃奏記光爭，以為「吏縱罪人，有常法，今更詆吳為不道，恐於法深。

translations.literal: Yannian thereupon memorialized to Guang contending, holding that "clerks who release guilty men have constant law; now again slandering Wu as 'non-dao' fears being deep in law.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian then wrote privately to Huo Guang: "There are standing statutes for officials who let criminals escape. To relabel Wu as guilty of 'immoral conduct' stretches the code past breaking.

sentences.zh: 又丞相素無所守持，而為好言於下，盡其素行也。

translations.literal: Again the chancellor has never held anything to guard, yet speaks kindly below—exhausting his plain conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover the chancellor has never been a rigid legalist; he pleads for others because that is simply his nature.

sentences.zh: 至擅召中二千石，甚無狀。

translations.literal: As to on his own summoning those of full two thousand piculs—very lacking in propriety.
translations.idiomatic: His unauthorized assembly was certainly irregular.

sentences.zh: 延年愚，以為丞相久故，及先帝用事，非有大故，不可棄也。

translations.literal: Yannian is foolish and thinks that because the chancellor is long-standing and old, and served the former emperor in power, without great cause he cannot be cast aside.
translations.idiomatic: Yet in my humble view he is a veteran who served the late emperor; without grave fault he should not be cast aside.

sentences.zh: 間者民頗言獄深，吏為峻詆，今丞相所議，又獄事也，如是以及丞相，恐不合眾心。

translations.literal: Recently the people rather speak of prisons deep, clerks making harsh slander; now what the chancellor deliberated is again a prison matter; if thus it reaches the chancellor, it fears not matching the multitude's heart.
translations.idiomatic: The people already complain that justice is too harsh and clerks too vindictive. To extend this quarrel to the chancellor himself would alienate public opinion.

sentences.zh: 群下言雚嘩，庶人私議，流言四布，延年竊重將軍失此名於天下也！」

translations.literal: The host below speaks clamorously; commoners privately discuss; drifting words spread in four directions—Yannian secretly fears the general will lose this name in the empire!"
translations.idiomatic: Murmurs are rising from every quarter. I would hate to see Your Lordship's good name tarnished throughout the realm for prosecuting Che Qianqiu!"

sentences.zh: 光以廷尉、少府弄法輕重，皆論棄市，而不以及丞相，終與相竟。

translations.literal: Guang held the commandant of justice and chamberlain toyed with law light and heavy; both were sentenced to display in the market, but did not reach to the chancellor; finally he completed the case with the minister.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang condemned Wang Ping and Xu Ren to public execution for twisting the statutes, but dropped the matter against the chancellor, letting the affair end there.

sentences.zh: 延年論議持平，合和朝廷，皆此類也。

translations.literal: Yannian's discussions held the balance even, harmonizing the court—all was of this kind.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian's habit of steering policy toward the moderate course and keeping the court in balance showed in episode after episode.

sentences.zh: 見國家承武帝奢侈師旅之後，數為大將軍光言：「年歲比不登，流民未盡還，宜修孝文明政，示以儉約寬和，順天心，說民意，年歲宜應。」

translations.literal: Seeing the state inheriting after Emperor Wu's extravagance and armies, several times he spoke to Grand General Guang: "Years in succession are not good harvests; wandering people have not all returned—it is fitting to cultivate Emperor Wen's enlightened government, show them frugal agreement lenient and harmonious, comply with Heaven's mind, please the people's intent—years and harvests should respond."
translations.idiomatic: Seeing the empire still reeling from Emperor Wu's wars and waste, he urged Huo Guang more than once: "Harvests have failed year after year and refugees have not all come home. We should revive the restrained, humane government of Emperor Wen, teach frugality and mercy, and align policy with Heaven and the common people—then the seasons will answer in kind."

sentences.zh: 光納其言，舉賢良，議罷酒榷、鹽、鐵，皆自延年發之。

translations.literal: Guang accepted his words, raised the worthy and good, deliberated abolishing wine monopoly, salt, and iron—all sprang from Yannian's initiating.
translations.idiomatic: Guang took his advice: the summons of worthy men, the debates on ending the wine monopoly and the salt and iron monopolies—all began with Yannian.

sentences.zh: 吏民上書言便宜，有異，輒下延年平處復奏。

translations.literal: Officials and people memorializing on conveniences, having differences, always sent down to Yannian to level-handle and memorialize again.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever officials or commoners submitted policy suggestions that sparked controversy, the drafts went to Yannian for adjudication and a follow-up memorial.

sentences.zh: 言可官試者，至為縣令，或丞相、御史除用，滿歲以狀聞，或抵其罪法，常與兩府及廷尉分章。

translations.literal: Those whose words could be officially tried, reached to being county magistrates, or chancellor and imperial counselor dismissed and employed; full year they reported the condition, or matched their crime to law—he constantly divided memorials with the two offices and the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: If a proposal merited a trial appointment, the man might be made county magistrate, or the chancellor and imperial counselor would assign him a post; after a full year his performance was reported, or he was charged if he had failed—Yannian routinely split this paperwork with the two chief ministries and the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 延年為人安和，備於諸事，久典朝政，上任信之，出即奉駕，入給事中，居九卿位十餘年，賞賜賂遺，訾數千萬。

translations.literal: Yannian as a person was peaceful and harmonious, complete in various affairs, long governing court policy; when the ruler went out he trusted him, going out then driving the carriage, entering giving palace attendant; dwelling in nine ministers rank more than ten years, gifts, bribes, and presents, property several tens of millions.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian was even-tempered and thorough. He ran central policy for years: when the emperor left the palace he rode as charioteer; when court sat he was at hand as palace attendant. More than a decade among the nine ministers brought him gifts and emoluments worth tens of millions.

sentences.zh: 霍光薨後，子禹與宗族謀反，誅。

translations.literal: After Huo Guang died, his son Yu with the clan plotted rebellion and was executed.
translations.idiomatic: After Huo Guang's death his son Huo Yu conspired with the clan and paid with his life.

sentences.zh: 上以延年霍氏舊人，欲退之，而丞相魏相奏延年素貴用事，官職多奸。

translations.literal: The ruler because Yannian was Huo clan's old man wished to retire him, but Chancellor Wei Xiang memorialized that Yannian had long been noble and in power, his offices many wicked.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, seeing Yannian as an old Huo partisan, meant to ease him out, but Chancellor Wei Xiang memorialized that Yannian had long abused high office and his conduct was riddled with corruption.

sentences.zh: 遣吏考案，但得苑馬多死，官奴婢乏衣食，延年坐免官，削戶二千。

translations.literal: He sent clerks to investigate; they only obtained that park horses many died, official slave girls lacked clothes and food; Yannian sat guilty and was dismissed from office, households cut two thousand.
translations.idiomatic: An inquiry turned up only neglected park horses and underfed government slaves—thin grounds—but Yannian was stripped of office and docked two thousand households of his fief.

sentences.zh: 後數月，復召拜為北地太守。

translations.literal: After several months, again summoned and appointed governor of Beidi.
translations.idiomatic: A few months later he was recalled and named governor of Beidi.

sentences.zh: 延年以故九卿外為邊吏，治郡不進，上以璽書讓延年。

translations.literal: Yannian because formerly nine ministers went out to be border clerk, governed the commandery without advancing; the ruler by sealed edict reproached Yannian.
translations.idiomatic: A former nine minister banished to a frontier post, he administered the commandery listlessly until the emperor sent a sealed edict rebuking him.

sentences.zh: 延年乃選用良吏，捕擊豪強，郡中清靜。

translations.literal: Yannian thereupon selected and employed good clerks, struck and captured the powerful and strong; within the commandery it was clear and quiet.
translations.idiomatic: Stung, he recruited able subordinates, smashed the local magnates, and brought order to the region.

sentences.zh: 居歲餘，上使謁者賜延年璽書，黃金二千斤，徙為西河太守，治甚有名。

translations.literal: Dwelling more than a year, the ruler sent a herald to grant Yannian a sealed edict, yellow gold two thousand jin, transferred him to be governor of Xihe; his governance had great reputation.
translations.idiomatic: After a year the court sent a herald with a commendatory edict and two thousand catties of gold, transferring him to Xihe, where his administration won wide praise.

sentences.zh: 五鳳中，征入為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the Wufeng years, summoned in as imperial counselor.
translations.idiomatic: During the Wufeng era (57–54 BCE) he was summoned to serve as imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 延年居父官府，不敢當舊位，坐臥皆易其處。

translations.literal: Yannian dwelt in his father's official residence, did not dare occupy the old seat; sitting and lying he all changed their places.
translations.idiomatic: He moved into his father's official mansion but refused to use the old reception rooms, changing every hall and chamber where Du Zhou had sat.

sentences.zh: 是時，四夷和，海內平，延年視事三歲，以老病乞骸骨，天子優之，使光祿大夫持節賜延年黃金百斤、酒，加致醫藥，延年遂稱病篤。

translations.literal: At this time the four barbarians were harmonious, within the seas peaceful; Yannian attended to affairs three years, and by old age and illness begged for his bones; the Son of Heaven treated him generously, sent the grand coachman with staff to grant Yannien yellow gold one hundred jin, wine, and in addition sent physicians and drugs; Yannien thereupon claimed illness was grave.
translations.idiomatic: The frontier was quiet and the realm at peace. After three years in office he cited age and ill health and asked leave to retire. The emperor treated him kindly, sending the grand coachman with a hundred catties of gold, wine, and physicians with medicines. Yannian then insisted his condition was critical.

sentences.zh: 賜安車駟馬，罷就第。

translations.literal: He was granted a secure carriage and four horses, dismissed to go to his mansion.
translations.idiomatic: He was awarded a comfortable carriage team and permission to withdraw to his private residence.

sentences.zh: 後數月薨，謚曰敬侯，子緩嗣。

translations.literal: After several months he died; posthumous title was Marquis Jing; his son Huan inherited.
translations.idiomatic: He died a few months later with the posthumous epithet "Respectful Marquis"; his son Du Huan succeeded him.

sentences.zh: 延年子 緩

translations.literal: Yannian's son: Huan.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian's son: Du Huan.

sentences.zh: 緩少為郎，本始中以校尉從蒲類將軍擊匈奴，還為諫大夫，遷上谷都尉，雁門太守。

translations.literal: Huan in youth was a gentleman; in Benshi he as colonel followed the Pulei general to strike the Xiongnu; returning he became remonstrance grandee, transferred to be commandant of Shanggu, governor of Yanmen.
translations.idiomatic: Du Huan began as a gentleman-attendant. In the Benshi era (73–70 BCE) he campaigned against the Xiongnu as a colonel under the Pulei general, then served as remonstrance grandee, commandant of Shanggu, and governor of Yanmen.

sentences.zh: 父延年薨，征視喪事，拜為太常，治諸陵縣，每冬月封具獄日，常去酒省食，官屬稱其有恩。

translations.literal: When father Yannian died, he was summoned to oversee funeral affairs, appointed grand master of ceremonies, governed the various mausoleum counties; each winter month when sealing and completing prison days, he always removed wine and reduced food; official subordinates praised him as having kindness.
translations.idiomatic: When Yannian died he was called to the capital to direct the obsequies and was appointed grand master of ceremonies in charge of the imperial tomb districts. Each winter, on the day capital cases were closed for the year, he abstained from wine and took spare meals; his staff praised his compassion.

sentences.zh: 元帝初即位，谷貴民流，永光中西羌反，緩輒上書入錢、谷以助用，前後數百萬。

translations.literal: When Emperor Yuan first took the throne, grain was expensive and people wandered; in Yongguang the western Qiang rebelled; Huan repeatedly memorialized entering cash and grain to assist use—front and rear several million.
translations.idiomatic: At the beginning of Yuandi's reign grain prices soared and refugees filled the roads. When the western Qiang rose in Yongguang, Huan repeatedly donated cash and grain to the treasury—hundreds of thousands over several years.

sentences.zh: 緩六弟，五人至大官，少弟熊歷五郡二千石、三州牧刺史，有能名，唯中弟欽官不至而最知名。

translations.literal: Huan had six younger brothers; five reached great office; the youngest brother Xiong passed through five commanderies as two thousand dan, three prefectures as shepherd or inspector, having a reputation for ability; only the middle brother Qin's office did not reach yet was most known by name.
translations.idiomatic: Huan had six younger brothers, five of whom rose to high rank. The youngest, Xiong, served as governor of five commanderies and shepherd or inspector of three provinces with a reputation for competence; only the fifth brother, Qin, never climbed so high yet became the most famous of the line.

sentences.zh: 緩弟 欽

translations.literal: Huan's younger brother: Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Huan's younger brother: Du Qin.

sentences.zh: 欽字子夏，少好經書，家富而目偏盲，故不好為吏。

translations.literal: Qin, courtesy Zixia, in youth loved the classics and documents; the family was rich but his eye was partly blind, therefore he did not like to be a clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Du Qin, courtesy Zixia, loved classical learning in his youth. His family was wealthy, but partial blindness in one eye left him unwilling to pursue a routine official career.

sentences.zh: 茂陵杜鄴與欽同姓字，俱以材能稱京師，故衣冠謂欽為「盲杜子夏」以相別。

translations.literal: Maoling Du Ye with Qin had the same surname and courtesy, both by talent and ability were praised in the capital; therefore caps and robes called Qin "Blind Du Zixia" to distinguish.
translations.idiomatic: Another Du Ye of Maoling shared his surname and courtesy name; both were known in Chang'an for their gifts, so the gentry dubbed our man "the blind Du Zixia" to tell the two apart.

sentences.zh: 欽惡以疾見詆，乃為小冠，高廣財二寸，由是京師更謂欽為「小冠杜子夏」，而鄴為「大冠杜子夏」云。

translations.literal: Qin hated being seen slandered by illness, and therefore made a small cap, high and wide only two cun; from this the capital further called Qin "Small-cap Du Zixia," while Ye was "Large-cap Du Zixia," it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Qin resented the nickname, so he took to wearing a tiny cap barely two inches high and wide; thereafter Chang'an called him "Little-Cap Du Zixia" and the other "Big-Cap Du Zixia."

sentences.zh: 時，帝舅大將軍王鳳以外戚輔政，求賢知自助。

translations.literal: At this time the emperor's maternal uncle Grand General Wang Feng by affinal kin assisted government, seeking worthies and knowledge to help himself.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile the emperor's uncle, Grand General Wang Feng, ruled as regent through his maternal connection and cast about for able men to strengthen his hand.

sentences.zh: 鳳父頃侯禁與欽兄緩相善，故鳳深知欽能，奏請欽為大將軍軍武庫令。

translations.literal: Feng's father Marquis Qing Jin was on good terms with Qin's elder brother Huan; therefore Feng deeply knew Qin's ability and memorialized requesting Qin as grand general army armory intendant.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Feng's late father, Marquis Qing Wang Jin, had been friendly with Du Huan, so Feng knew Qin's talents well and had him appointed intendant of the arsenal on the general's staff.

sentences.zh: 職閒無事，欽所好也。

translations.literal: The duty was idle without affairs—what Qin loved.
translations.idiomatic: The post was a sinecure—exactly what Qin wanted.

sentences.zh: 鳳白之太后，太后以為故事無有。

translations.literal: Feng reported it to the empress dowager; the empress dowager thought by precedent there was none.
translations.idiomatic: When Feng laid the memorial before the empress dowager, she replied that no precedent existed for what he proposed.

sentences.zh: 欽復重言：「《詩》云：『殷監不遠，在夏後氏之世』。

translations.literal: Qin again emphasized saying, "The Songs says, 'Yin's mirror is not far—it is in the Xia royal house's generation."
translations.idiomatic: Qin pressed his case again, quoting the Book of Odes: "Yin's mirror is not far off—it lies in the house of Xia."

sentences.zh: 刺戒者至迫近，而省聽者常怠忽，可不慎哉！

translations.literal: Those who prick and warn are extremely close, yet those who listen are always negligent—can one not be cautious!
translations.idiomatic: The warning voices are close at hand, yet rulers grow deaf to them—can we afford to be careless?

sentences.zh: 前言九女，略陳其禍福，甚可悼懼，竊恐將軍不深留意。

translations.literal: The former words on nine daughters, briefly displaying their disaster and fortune, were very worthy of grief and fear; I privately fear the general does not deeply attend.
translations.idiomatic: My earlier memorial on the nine imperial concubines sketched their blessings and curses in frightening detail; I fear Your Lordship has not weighed it seriously enough.

sentences.zh: 后妃之制，夭壽治亂存亡之端也。

translations.literal: The system of empress and consorts is the tip of early death, long life, order, chaos, survival, and destruction.
translations.idiomatic: The institution of empress and harem is where the fate of dynasties turns—whether rulers die young or live long, whether the realm thrives or falls.

sentences.zh: 跡三代之季世，覽宗、宣之饗國，察近屬之符驗，禍敗曷常不由女德？

translations.literal: Tracing the three dynasties' final generations, viewing Zong and Xuan's enjoying the state, examining near kin's omens and verification—disaster and defeat—how was it ever not from women's virtue?
translations.idiomatic: Trace the last kings of the three ancient dynasties, read how the great affinal clans fared from Emperor Xuan through Emperor Yuan, then look at the omens among your own kin: has catastrophe ever come from anywhere but the inner quarters?

sentences.zh: 是以佩玉晏鳴，《關雎》歎之，知好色之伐性短年，離制度之生無厭，天下將蒙化，陵夷而成俗也。

translations.literal: Therefore jade pendants delayed the dawn crowing, Guanju sighs at it, knowing that love of beauty harms nature and shortens years, that departing from the system breeds never satiated—under Heaven will receive transformation, decline and become custom.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the ritual bells that slowed the morning audience and the sigh of "Guanju" over unchecked passion—excess in the harem wastes the body and shortens life; once the proper numbers are abandoned, desire knows no limit, and the whole realm takes its tone from the palace until decadence becomes habit.

sentences.zh: 故詠淑女，幾以配上，忠孝之篤，仁厚之作也。

translations.literal: Therefore singing the fair lady, hoping to match the superior, is the thickness of loyalty and filiality, the work of benevolence and generosity.
translations.idiomatic: That is why the classic praises the modest bride who fits her lord: it is the root of loyal service and filial devotion, the very embodiment of humane governance.

sentences.zh: 夫君親壽尊，國家治安，誠臣子至願，所當勉之也。

translations.literal: Now if lord and parent live long and honored, state and family are peaceful and secure—this is truly what ministers and sons most desire, what they ought to strive for.
translations.idiomatic: Long life and high honor for ruler and father, peace for state and family—that is every loyal subject's prayer and his proper goal.

sentences.zh: 《易》曰：『正其本，萬物理。』

translations.literal: The Changes says, "Correct its root, and the myriad things obtain their pattern."
translations.idiomatic: As the Classic of Changes puts it, "Set the root straight and the myriad things fall into order."

sentences.zh: 凡事論有疑未可立行者，求之往古則典刑無，考之來今則吉凶同，卒搖易之則民心惑，若是者誠難施也。

translations.literal: Whenever in deliberating affairs there is doubt and what cannot be established and carried out, seeking it in the past ancient then models and punishments are not; examining it in coming present then good and ill are the same; finally shaking and changing it then the people's hearts are doubtful—such as this is truly hard to apply.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever policy is uncertain, looking backward yields no clear precedent and looking around shows only mixed results; flip-flopping further confuses the people—such measures are nearly impossible to enforce.

sentences.zh: 今九女之制，合於往古，無害於今，不逆於民心，至易行也，行之至有福也，將軍輔政而不蚤定，非天下之所望也。

translations.literal: Now the nine-daughters system matches the past ancient, harms not the present, does not oppose the people's heart, is extremely easy to carry out; carrying it out reaches greatest blessing; the general assists government yet does not early fix it—not what the empire hopes for.
translations.idiomatic: The ancient rule limiting the harem to nine secondary consorts fits antiquity, injures nothing today, and offends no popular sentiment; it could be enacted tomorrow to the empire's great good. Yet you govern as regent and still delay—this is not what the realm expects of you.

sentences.zh: 唯將軍信臣子之願，念《關雎》之思，逮委政之隆，及始初清明，為漢家建無窮之基，誠難以忽，不可以遴。」

translations.literal: Only may the general trust your servant-son's wish, think on Guanju's thought, seize the height of delegated government's vigor, and while the beginning is still clear and bright, for the Han house establish inexhaustible foundation—truly hard to neglect, cannot be put aside."
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to heed a loyal subject, recall the lesson of "Guanju," seize this moment of paramount authority while your judgment is still lucid, and lay an unshakable foundation for Han. The matter is too grave to shrug off or postpone."

sentences.zh: 鳳不能自立法度，循故事而已。

translations.literal: Feng could not himself establish law degrees; he followed precedent only.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Feng lacked the courage to innovate; he hid behind precedent.

sentences.zh: 會皇太后女弟司馬君力與欽兄子私通，事上聞，欽慚懼，乞骸骨去。

translations.literal: It happened that the empress dowager's younger sister Sima Junli had private intercourse with Qin's elder brother's son; the affair ascended to hearing; Qin was ashamed and afraid, begged bones and departed.
translations.idiomatic: Then it came out that the empress dowager's younger sister Sima Junli had carried on an affair with Qin's nephew. Qin, mortified and terrified, resigned his post.

sentences.zh: 後有日蝕、地震之變，詔舉賢良方正能直言士，合陽侯梁放舉欽。

translations.literal: Afterward there were solar eclipse and earthquake changes; an edict ordered raising the worthy and upright who could speak directly; Marquis of Heyang Liang Fang raised Qin.
translations.idiomatic: Later, after solar eclipses and earthquakes, the throne called for outspoken men of integrity. Liang Fang, the marquis of Heyang, nominated Du Qin.

sentences.zh: 欽上對曰：「陛下畏天命，悼變異，延見公卿，舉直言之士，將以求天心，跡得失也。

translations.literal: Qin submitted reply, saying, "Your Majesty fears Heaven's command, grieves at changes and prodigies, extends audience to dukes and ministers, raises straight-speaking knights—you will thereby seek Heaven's mind and trace gain and loss."
translations.idiomatic: Du Qin answered the rescript: "Your Majesty stands in awe of Heaven, mourns these omens, has opened the court to ministers, and summoned blunt speakers because you wish to read the mind of Heaven and learn where government has erred."

sentences.zh: 臣欽愚戇，經術淺薄，不足以奉大對。

translations.literal: Your servant Qin is foolish and blunt; his classical learning is shallow and thin—not enough to undertake this great reply.
translations.idiomatic: Your servant Du Qin is dull-witted and poorly versed in the classics—hardly fit to answer so weighty an examination.

sentences.zh: 臣聞日蝕、地震，陽微陰盛也。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that solar eclipse and earthquake mean yang is slight and yin is abundant.
translations.idiomatic: I have read that eclipses and earthquakes betoken feeble yang and rampant yin.

sentences.zh: 臣者，君之陰也；

translations.literal: The minister is the ruler's yin;
translations.idiomatic: Ministers are the yin counterpart to their sovereign;

sentences.zh: 子者，父之陰也；

translations.literal: the son is the father's yin;
translations.idiomatic: sons, to their fathers;

sentences.zh: 妻者，夫之陰也；

translations.literal: the wife is the husband's yin;
translations.idiomatic: wives, to their husbands;

sentences.zh: 夷狄者，中國之陰也。

translations.literal: the barbarians are the Central States' yin.
translations.idiomatic: and the barbarians, to the Chinese heartland.

sentences.zh: 《春秋》日蝕三十六，地震五，或夷狄侵中國，或政權在臣下，或婦乘夫，或臣子背君父，事雖不同，其類一也。

translations.literal: The Spring and Autumn records thirty-six solar eclipses and five earthquakes—sometimes barbarians invaded the Central States, sometimes government power lay under ministers, sometimes wives rode atop husbands, sometimes sons and ministers betrayed lord and father; though the affairs differed, the category was one.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn records thirty-six eclipses and five earthquakes: some coincided with barbarian invasions, some with ministerial dictatorship, some with wives dominating husbands, some with subjects betraying their lords—different events, one pattern.

sentences.zh: 臣竊觀人事以考變異，則本朝大臣無不自安之人，外戚親屬無乖刺之心，關東諸侯無強大之國，三垂蠻夷無逆理之節；

translations.literal: Your servant privately views human affairs to examine prodigies—then this court's great ministers have none who are not self-secure; affinal kin have no perverse-thorn hearts; east of the Pass the feudal lords have no mighty great states; on the three frontiers the barbarians have no contrary-to-pattern conduct;
translations.idiomatic: When I weigh human affairs against these omens, I find no minister at court nursing sedition, no imperial in-laws nursing grudges, no eastern kingdom strong enough to challenge the throne, no frontier tribe openly defying the moral order;

sentences.zh: 殆為後宮。

translations.literal: probably it is the rear palace.
translations.idiomatic: the source must lie in the harem.

sentences.zh: 何以言之？

translations.literal: How do I say it?
translations.idiomatic: Why do I say so?

sentences.zh: 日以戊申蝕。

translations.literal: The sun was eclipsed on wushen day.
translations.idiomatic: The eclipse fell on the day wushen.

sentences.zh: 時加未。

translations.literal: The hour added was wei.
translations.idiomatic: The hour was wei.

sentences.zh: 戊未，土也。

translations.literal: Wu and wei are earth.
translations.idiomatic: Both wu and wei belong to the element earth.

sentences.zh: 土者，中宮之部也。

translations.literal: Earth is the department of the central palace.
translations.idiomatic: Earth governs the central palace—the empress's domain.

sentences.zh: 其夜地震未央宮殿中，此必適妾將有爭寵相害而為患者，唯陛下深戒之。

translations.literal: That night the earth shook within Weiyang Palace—this must mean secondary consorts will contend for favor and harm one another and become a scourge; only may Your Majesty deeply guard against it.
translations.idiomatic: That very night the earth shook inside Weiyang Palace. The omen points to rival concubines contending for your favor and doing one another harm. I beg Your Majesty to take it to heart.

sentences.zh: 變感以類相應，人事失於下，變象見於上。

translations.literal: Prodigies respond by category to one another; human affairs miss below, prodigy images appear above.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven answers human misconduct in kind: error below, portent above.

sentences.zh: 能應之以德，則異咎消亡；

translations.literal: If able to respond to them with virtue, then strange blame is extinguished;
translations.idiomatic: Meet them with true virtue and the ill omens will fade;

sentences.zh: 不能應之以善，則禍敗至。

translations.literal: if not able to respond to them with goodness, then disaster and defeat arrive.
translations.idiomatic: fail to reform, and ruin follows.

sentences.zh: 高宗遭雊雉之戒，飭己正事，享百年之壽，殷道復興，要在所以應之。

translations.literal: Gaozong received the pheasant's crowing warning, corrected himself and set affairs right, enjoyed a hundred years' life, Yin's way again rose—the crux lies in how one responds.
translations.idiomatic: King Gaozong of Yin heeded the warning of the crowing pheasants, set his house in order, lived a long life, and restored the dynasty—everything turns on how a ruler answers Heaven.

sentences.zh: 應之非誠不立，非信不行。

translations.literal: To respond, without sincerity it does not stand; without trust it does not proceed.
translations.idiomatic: Such a response must be sincere to endure and trustworthy to succeed.

sentences.zh: 宋景公，小國之諸侯耳，有不忍移禍之誠，出人君之言三，熒惑為之退捨。

translations.literal: Duke Jing of Song, a small state's feudal lord, had the sincerity of not shifting disaster; he uttered the lord's words three times, and the Sparkling Deluder for him withdrew and halted.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Jing of Song was lord of a minor state, yet his unwillingness to shift calamity onto his subjects was so genuine that he thrice spoke as a true sovereign should—and Mars changed its course for him.

sentences.zh: 以陛下聖明，內推至誠，深思天變，何應而不感？

translations.literal: With Your Majesty's sagely clarity, inwardly pushing utmost sincerity, deeply pondering Heaven's changes—what response would not be moved?
translations.idiomatic: Your Majesty is wise enough to search your heart, ponder these signs in utmost good faith, and move Heaven itself.

sentences.zh: 何搖而不動？

translations.literal: What shaking would not be stirred?
translations.idiomatic: What stubborn ill could then resist you?

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『仁遠乎哉！』

translations.literal: Confucius said, "Is benevolence far away?"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius asked, "Is humanity really so far away?"

sentences.zh: 唯陛下正後妾，抑女寵，防奢泰，去佚游，躬節儉，親萬事，數御安車，由輦道，親二宮之饔膳，致晨昏之定省。

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty correct empress and secondary consorts, restrain women's favor, guard against extravagance and excess, remove wandering pleasure, personally practice moderation, personally attend to the myriad affairs, frequently ride the secure carriage, by the carriage track, personally attend the two palaces' feasts, extend morning and evening's fixed inquiries.
translations.idiomatic: Correct the harem hierarchy, curb excessive favor to women, shun luxury and idle sport, practice personal frugality, mind the myriad details of government yourself, ride often in the modest state carriage along the palace carriage ways, attend your mother and aunt in the two palaces in person, and perform the morning and evening courtesies of a filial son.

sentences.zh: 如此，即堯、舜不足與比隆，咎異何足消滅？

translations.literal: If thus, then Yao and Shun are not enough to compare in loftiness—how could strange blame suffice to be extinguished?
translations.idiomatic: Do this, and you will surpass even Yao and Shun—what omen could stand against you?

sentences.zh: 如不留聽於庶事，不論材而授位，殫天下之財以奉淫侈，匱萬姓之力以從耳目，近諂諛之人而遠公方，信讒賊之臣以誅忠良，賢俊失在巖穴，大臣怨於不以，雖無變異、社稷之憂也。

translations.literal: If you do not retain listening in the myriad affairs, do not discuss talent and grant office, exhaust the empire's wealth to serve dissolute extravagance, drain the myriad surnames' strength to follow ears and eyes, draw near to fawning sycophants and distance the public and upright, trust slanderous wicked ministers to execute the loyal and good, so that worthy and outstanding are lost in cliff caves and great ministers resent not being used—although there are no prodigies, there is worry for altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: But if you ignore the business of government, hand posts to unfit men, squander the treasury on debauchery, grind the people down to amuse your senses, flatterers at your elbow while honest men stand far off, believe slander and kill the loyal until talent hides in the hills and great ministers nurse resentment—then even without eclipses or tremors, the altars of state are already in peril.

sentences.zh: 天下至大，萬事至眾，祖業至重，誠不可以佚豫為，不可以奢泰持也。

translations.literal: The empire is greatest, the myriad affairs most numerous, the ancestral enterprise weightiest—truly one cannot act with idle ease, cannot hold with extravagance and excess.
translations.idiomatic: The realm is vast, its business endless, and the patrimony you hold is heavy beyond measure: you cannot rule by self-indulgence or maintain it through excess.

sentences.zh: 唯陛下忍無益之欲，以全眾庶之命。

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty endure and set aside profitless desires, thereby to preserve the multitude of commoners' lives.
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to renounce pleasures that profit nothing and so preserve the lives of your people.

sentences.zh: 臣欽愚戇，言不足采。」

translations.literal: Your servant Qin is foolish and blunt; his words are not worth gathering."
translations.idiomatic: Your foolish servant has said more than his deserts; whether any of it merits heed is for Your Majesty alone to judge."

sentences.zh: 其夏，上盡召直言之士詣白虎殿對策，策曰：「天地之道何貴？

translations.literal: That summer the ruler fully summoned straight-speaking knights to proceed to the White Tiger Hall to answer policy questions; the question said, "Of the Way of Heaven and earth what is honored?
translations.idiomatic: That summer the emperor convened every outspoken scholar at the White Tiger Hall for the policy examination. The question read: "What does the Way of Heaven and earth honor above all?

sentences.zh: 王者之法何如？

translations.literal: The king's model—how is it?
translations.idiomatic: What should be the model for a true king?

sentences.zh: 《六經》之義何上？

translations.literal: The meaning of the Six Classics—what is highest?
translations.idiomatic: What do the Six Classics rank highest?

sentences.zh: 人之行何先？

translations.literal: Among men's conduct what comes first?
translations.idiomatic: In human conduct, what comes first?

sentences.zh: 取人之術何以？

translations.literal: The art of taking men—by what?
translations.idiomatic: By what art does one choose officials?

sentences.zh: 當世之治何務？

translations.literal: The ordering of the present age—what is the task?
translations.idiomatic: What is the urgent task of government today?

sentences.zh: 各以經對。」

translations.literal: Each use the classics to answer."
translations.idiomatic: Let each answer from the classics."

sentences.zh: 欽對曰：「臣聞天道貴信，地道貴貞；

translations.literal: Qin replied, saying, "Your servant has heard the Way of Heaven honors trust, the Way of earth honors constancy;
translations.idiomatic: Du Qin replied: "Heaven esteems good faith; earth esteems constancy.

sentences.zh: 不信不貞，萬物不生。

translations.literal: Without trust and without constancy, the myriad things are not born.
translations.idiomatic: Without them nothing lives.

sentences.zh: 生，天地之所貴也。

translations.literal: Birth is what Heaven and earth honor.
translations.idiomatic: Life itself is what Heaven and earth most cherish.

sentences.zh: 王者承天地之所生，理而成之，昆蟲草木靡不得其所。

translations.literal: The king receives what Heaven and earth give birth to, orders and completes it—insects and grass and trees none fail to obtain their proper place.
translations.idiomatic: The king takes what Heaven and earth nurture and brings all creatures, down to the least insect and blade of grass, to their proper estate.

sentences.zh: 王者法天地，非仁無以廣施，非義無以正身；

translations.literal: The king models Heaven and earth; without benevolence there is no way broadly to bestow; without righteousness there is no way to correct the self;
translations.idiomatic: He takes Heaven and earth as his pattern: without humanity he cannot spread blessings abroad; without righteousness he cannot set his own person straight;

sentences.zh: 克己就義，恕以及人，《六經》之所上也。

translations.literal: conquering self to approach righteousness, extending forgiveness to reach others—this is what the Six Classics place highest.
translations.idiomatic: self-discipline in the name of duty, forgiveness extended to others—that is what the Six Classics rank above all else.

sentences.zh: 不孝，則事君不忠，蒞官不敬，戰陳無勇，朋友不信。

translations.literal: Without filiality, then serving the lord is not loyal, attending office is not respectful, in battle array there is no courage, toward friends there is no trust.
translations.idiomatic: Without filial piety there can be no loyal service to the throne, no reverent discharge of office, no courage in battle, no good faith among friends.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『孝無終始，而患不及者，未之有也。』

translations.literal: Confucius said, "Filiality has no beginning or end, yet those who worry they cannot keep pace—there has never been such."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "Filial duty is a road without end, yet no one who sets foot on it need ever fear he cannot keep up."

sentences.zh: 孝，人行之所先也。

translations.literal: Filiality is what men's conduct puts first.
translations.idiomatic: Filial conduct must come first in every life.

sentences.zh: 觀本行於鄉黨，考功能於官職，達觀其所舉，富觀其所予，窮觀其所不為，乏觀其所不取，近觀其所為主，遠觀其所主。

translations.literal: Observe the root conduct in village and hamlet, examine function and ability in office; when prosperous observe whom he raises, when rich observe what he gives; when poor observe what he will not do, when exhausted observe what he will not take; at near observe whom he serves as host, at far observe whom he serves as lord.
translations.idiomatic: Watch a man among his neighbors, test him in office; when he is rich, see whom he promotes and what he gives away; when he is poor, see what baseness he refuses and what bribe he will not touch; close at hand note whom he treats as patron, from afar whom he chooses as master.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『視其所以，觀其所由，察其所安，人焉瘦哉？』

translations.literal: Confucius said, "Look at what he does, observe by what means he does it, examine where he rests at ease—how can a man hide?"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "Study his acts, trace his motives, note where he is content—then how can character stay hidden?"

sentences.zh: 取人之術也。

translations.literal: This is the art of taking men.
translations.idiomatic: That is the art of choosing men for office.

sentences.zh: 殷因於夏尚質，周因於殷尚文，今漢家承周、秦之敝，宜抑文尚質，廢奢長儉，表實去偽。

translations.literal: Yin took over from Xia and honored substance; Zhou took over from Yin and honored pattern; now the Han house receives Zhou and Qin's exhaustion—it is fitting to restrain pattern and honor substance, abolish extravagance and lengthen frugality, display substance and remove falsity.
translations.idiomatic: Yin succeeded Xia and valued plain substance; Zhou succeeded Yin and valued ritual refinement. Han inherits the exhaustion of both Qin and Zhou: we should curb empty display, return to simplicity, shun waste, encourage thrift, and prize the genuine over the counterfeit.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰『惡紫之奪硃』，當世治之所務也。

translations.literal: Confucius said "I hate that purple takes vermilion"—this is what the ordering of the present age must attend to.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said he hated how purple crowds out true red—that is the reform our age most needs.

sentences.zh: 臣竊有所憂，言之則拂心逆指，不言則漸日長，為禍不細，然小臣不敢廢道而求從，違忠而耦意。

translations.literal: Your servant privately has something he worries about: to speak it brushes the heart and reverses the intent; not to speak it then gradually lengthens day by day, becoming a scourge not small—yet a small minister dare not abandon the Way to seek compliance, betray loyalty to pair with the ruler's mind.
translations.idiomatic: I have a further worry: to voice it offends Your Majesty, yet silence lets the evil grow day by day into no small harm. Still, a petty official may not abandon the right path to curry favor or choose treachery over loyalty.

sentences.zh: 臣聞玩色無厭，必生好憎之心；

translations.literal: Your servant has heard that playing with beauty without satiety must give birth to love-and-hate mind;
translations.idiomatic: Endless dalliance with women breeds love and hate;

sentences.zh: 好憎之心生，則愛寵偏於一人；

translations.literal: when love-and-hate mind is born, then favored love leans to one person;
translations.idiomatic: love and hate concentrate favor on a single face;

sentences.zh: 愛寵偏於一人，則繼嗣之路不廣，而嫉妒之心興矣。

translations.literal: when favored love leans to one person, then the heir's road is not broad and jealous mind rises.
translations.idiomatic: and when favor leans on one alone, the succession narrows and jealousy flares among the rest.

sentences.zh: 如此，則匹婦之說，不可勝也。

translations.literal: If thus, then the talk of common wives cannot be overcome.
translations.idiomatic: Then the whispering of the women's quarters becomes impossible to silence.

sentences.zh: 唯陛下純德普施，無慾是從，此則眾庶咸說，繼嗣日廣，而海內長安。

translations.literal: Only may Your Majesty pure virtue broadly bestow, follow only no-desire—then the multitude of commoners will all be pleased, heirs will daily broaden, and within the seas there will be long peace.
translations.idiomatic: Spread your pure virtue evenly, master your desires, and the people will rejoice, the imperial line will flourish, and the realm will know lasting peace.

sentences.zh: 萬事之是非何足備言！」

translations.literal: Of the myriad affairs' right and wrong—how is it worth fully speaking!"
translations.idiomatic: As for every other question of right and wrong—words are hardly needed!"

sentences.zh: 欽以前事病，賜帛罷，後為議郎，復以病免。

translations.literal: Qin because of the former affair was ill, was granted silk and dismissed; afterward became consultant grandee, again because of illness was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: After the scandal Du Qin pleaded illness, received a gift of silk, and left office. He was later made a consultant grandee but again resigned on grounds of ill health.

sentences.zh: 征詣大將軍莫府，國家政謀，鳳常與欽慮之。

translations.literal: Summoned to the grand general's headquarters—state policy and stratagem—Feng constantly deliberated them with Qin.
translations.idiomatic: He was then summoned to Grand General Wang Feng's headquarters, where Feng habitually discussed state strategy with him.

sentences.zh: 數稱達名士王駿、韋安世、王延世等，救解馮野王、王尊、胡常之罪過，及繼功臣絕世，填撫四夷，當世善政，多出於欽者。

translations.literal: Repeatedly he praised and advanced noted gentlemen Wang Jun, Wei Anshi, Wang Yanshi, and others, rescued and explained the faults of Feng Yewang, Wang Zun, and Hu Chang, and as to continuing meritorious ministers' cut-off lines, filling and soothing the four barbarians—good government of the age mostly came from Qin.
translations.idiomatic: He repeatedly praised worthy men such as Wang Jun, Wei Anshi, and Wang Yanshi to high office, cleared Feng Yewang, Wang Zun, and Hu Chang of unjust charges, restored titles to meritorious families whose lines had failed, and soothed the frontier tribes—most of the era's sound policies owed something to Du Qin.

sentences.zh: 見鳳專政泰重，戒之曰：「昔周公身有至聖之德，屬有叔父之親，而成王有獨見之明，無信讒之聽，然管、蔡流言而周公懼。

translations.literal: Seeing Feng monopolized government too heavily, he warned him, saying, "Formerly the Duke of Zhou personally had utmost sage virtue, in kinship had uncle relation, yet King Cheng had alone-seeing clarity, had no listening to slander—still Guan and Cai spread drifting words and the Duke of Zhou feared.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing Wang Feng's grip on power grow oppressive, Du Qin warned him: The Duke of Zhou was the paragon of sagehood and the king's uncle, and King Cheng was a discerning sovereign who refused slander—yet the rumor spread by Guan and Cai still struck terror into the duke.

sentences.zh: 穰侯，昭王之舅也，權重於秦，威震鄰敵，有旦莫偃伏之愛，心不介然有間，然范雎起徒步，由異國，無雅信，開一朝之說，而穰侯就封。

translations.literal: Marquis Rang was King Zhao of Qin maternal uncle; his weight of power was in Qin, his awesomeness shook neighboring enemies; he had morning-and-evening couch-bending love, his mind was not stuck with a gap—yet Fan Ju rose from walking the roads, came from a different state, had no long-standing trust, opened one morning persuasion, and Marquis Rang went to his fief.
translations.idiomatic: Marquis Rang, uncle to King Zhao of Qin, dominated that state and awed its neighbors; he shared the king's pillow and board without a shadow between them—yet Fan Ju, a stranger on foot, won a single hearing and sent Rang packing to his domain.

sentences.zh: 及近者武安侯之見退，三事之跡，相去各數百歲，若合符節，甚不可不察。

translations.literal: As to the near case of the Marquis of Wu an being dismissed—the three affairs traces, separated by several hundred years each, match tally and seal—very much cannot fail to be examined.
translations.idiomatic: Add the recent fall of the Marquis of Wu'an: three lessons centuries apart that fit together like matching tallies. They demand your deepest reflection.

sentences.zh: 願將軍由周公之謙懼，損穰侯之威，放武安之欲，毋使范雎之徒得間其說。」

translations.literal: I wish the general would take the Duke of Zhou humble fear, diminish Marquis Rang awesomeness, release the Marquis of Wu an desires, and not let a Fan Ju class obtain a gap for his persuasion.
translations.idiomatic: I urge you to keep the Duke of Zhou's humility, shed Marquis Rang's arrogance, and curb the appetites that ruined the Marquis of Wu'an—lest another Fan Ju find a crack through which to speak.

sentences.zh: 頃之，復日蝕，京兆尹王章上封事求見，果言鳳專權蔽主之過，宜廢勿用，以應天變。

translations.literal: Before long, again solar eclipse; Metropolitan Governor Wang Zhang submitted a sealed memorial requesting audience, and indeed spoke of Feng monopolizing power and covering the ruler fault—it was fitting to dismiss and not use him, to respond to Heaven change.
translations.idiomatic: Soon another eclipse darkened the sun. Wang Zhang, governor of the capital, sealed a memorial and begged an audience, denouncing Wang Feng for monopolizing power and blinding the throne. He urged that Feng be removed to answer Heaven.

sentences.zh: 於是天子感悟，召見章，與議，欲退鳳。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven was moved and awakened, summoned and saw Wang Zhang, and with him deliberated, wishing to retire Feng.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was stirred, received Wang Zhang in audience, and began to consider dismissing Feng.

sentences.zh: 鳳甚憂懼，欽令鳳上疏謝罪，乞骸骨，文指甚哀。

translations.literal: Feng was greatly worried and afraid; Qin ordered Feng to submit a memorial apologizing for guilt, begging bones; the text intent was very piteous.
translations.idiomatic: Feng was terrified. Du Qin had him memorialize an abject apology and offer to resign—the wording was heart-rending.

sentences.zh: 太后涕泣為不食。

translations.literal: The empress dowager wept tears and for him did not eat.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager wept and refused her meals for his sake.

sentences.zh: 上少而親倚鳳，亦不忍廢，復起鳳就位。

translations.literal: The ruler from youth had been intimate with and relied on Feng, also could not bear to dismiss him, again raised Feng to take his position.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor, who had leaned on Feng since boyhood, could not bear to cast him off and restored him to power.

sentences.zh: 鳳心慚，稱病篤，欲遂退。

translations.literal: Feng heart was ashamed; he claimed illness was grave, wishing thereupon to retire.
translations.idiomatic: Chastened, Feng pleaded grave illness and tried to withdraw.

sentences.zh: 欽復說之曰：「將軍深悼輔政十年，變異不已，故乞骸骨，歸咎於身，刻己自責，至誠動眾，愚知莫不感傷。

translations.literal: Qin again persuaded him, saying, "The general deeply grieves that in ten years of assisting government prodigies have not ceased—therefore begging bones, returning blame to the self, carving the self and blaming the self—utmost sincerity moves the multitude; foolish and wise none are not moved and wounded.
translations.idiomatic: Du Qin urged him again: "Your memorial blames yourself for ten years of ill omens under your regency and asks to retire—such self-reproach moves every heart, high or low.

sentences.zh: 雖然，是無屬之臣，執進退之分，絜其去就之節者耳，非主上所以待將軍，非將軍所以報主上也。

translations.literal: Although it is so, this is the conduct of a minister without attachment, who holds the division of advance and retreat and marks the knot of going and staying—no more. It is not what the ruler uses to treat the general, nor what the general uses to repay the ruler."
translations.idiomatic: Yet that is the language of a disposable clerk who minds only his own exit—hardly what a loyal minister owes his sovereign, or a sovereign owes the pillar of his state."

sentences.zh: 昔周公雖老，猶在京師，明不離成周，示不忘王室也。

translations.literal: Formerly although the Duke of Zhou was old, he still was in the capital region, making clear he did not leave Cheng Zhou, showing he did not forget the royal house.
translations.idiomatic: The Duke of Zhou stayed in the capital in old age to show he never abandoned the royal city or the king.

sentences.zh: 仲山父異姓之臣，無親於宣，就封於齊，猶歎息永懷，宿夜徘徊，不忍遠去，況將軍之於主上，主上之與將軍哉！

translations.literal: Zhong Shanfu was a minister of different surname, without closeness to Xuan, yet when he went to his fief in Qi still sighed long thoughts, lodged night wandering, could not bear to go far—how much more the general toward the lord above, the lord above toward the general!
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Shanfu, no kin to King Xuan, still lingered in sorrow when posted to Qi—how much more should you, bound to the emperor by blood and office?"

sentences.zh: 夫欲天下治安變異之意，莫有將軍，主上照然知之，故攀援不遣，《書》稱『公毋困我！』

translations.literal: In desiring the empire peace and security and prodigies meaning, none compares to the general; the ruler clearly knows it—therefore he pulls the rope and does not send away; the Documents says Duke do not distress me.
translations.idiomatic: No one else can steady the realm or read these omens as you can, and the emperor knows it—hence he clings to you and will not let go. The Documents cry, "My uncle, do not abandon me!"

sentences.zh: 唯將軍不為四國流言自疑於成王，以固至忠。」

translations.literal: Only may the general not because of the four states drifting words doubt yourself toward King Cheng, thereby to solidify utmost loyalty.
translations.idiomatic: Do not, like the Duke of Zhou, almost let slander from the four quarters shake your faith in your king—hold fast to perfect loyalty.

sentences.zh: 鳳復起視事。

translations.literal: Feng again rose and attended to affairs.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Feng returned to duty.

sentences.zh: 上令尚書劾奏京兆尹章，章死詔獄。

translations.literal: The ruler ordered the Masters of Writing to impeach and memorialize Metropolitan Governor Wang Zhang; Wang Zhang died in imperial-order prison.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then ordered the Masters of Writing to impeach Wang Zhang, who died in the imperial prison.

sentences.zh: 語在《元後傳》。

translations.literal: The words are in the biography of Empress Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: The full story is told in the biography of Empress Yuan.

sentences.zh: 章既死，眾庶冤之，以譏朝廷。

translations.literal: When Wang Zhang had died, the multitude of commoners thought him wronged, thereby to ridicule the court.
translations.idiomatic: The people believed Wang Zhang had been wronged, and the court became a laughingstock.

sentences.zh: 欽欲救其過，復說鳳曰：「京兆尹章所坐事密，吏民見章素好言事，以為不坐官職，疑其以日蝕見對有所言也。

translations.literal: Qin wished to rescue his fault, again persuaded Feng, saying, What Metropolitan Governor Wang Zhang sat guilty of was secret; officials and people saw Wang Zhang had always loved to speak on affairs, and thought he did not sit guilty of office rank, suspected that because of the solar eclipse his reply to policy had something he said.
translations.idiomatic: Du Qin, hoping to repair the damage, urged Feng: Wang Zhang real offense was never made public. The capital supposes that because he always spoke boldly on policy, he lost his life not for corruption but for what he said about the eclipse.

sentences.zh: 假令章內有所犯，雖陷正法，事不暴揚，自京師不曉，況於遠方。

translations.literal: Suppose Wang Zhang inwardly had something he violated; although sunk in correct law, the affair was not violently displayed—from the capital they did not understand, how much more the distant regions.
translations.idiomatic: Even if he were guilty of some private crime, a quiet execution would have left the capital guessing, to say nothing of the provinces.

sentences.zh: 恐天下不知章實有罪，而以為坐言事也。

translations.literal: I fear the empire does not know Wang Zhang in fact had guilt, and thinks he sat guilty for speaking on affairs.
translations.idiomatic: I fear the realm will conclude he was executed for honest counsel, not for proven guilt.

sentences.zh: 如是，塞爭引之原，損寬明之德。

translations.literal: If thus, then it blocks the spring of contention and citation, injures the virtue of breadth and clarity.
translations.idiomatic: That would choke off frank advice and tarnish your reputation for fairness.

sentences.zh: 欽愚以為宜因章事舉直言極諫，並見郎從官展盡其章，加於往前，以明示四方，使天下咸知主上聖明，不以言罪下也。

translations.literal: Qin foolishly thinks it is fitting to take Wang Zhang affair to raise straight speech and utmost remonstrance, together see gentlemen-attendants and follower officials unfold their memorials to the full, add beyond what went before, thereby to make clear to the four quarters, cause the empire all to know the ruler is sagely clear and does not by words blame subordinates.
translations.idiomatic: I suggest you use this case to invite the harshest criticism: let every gentleman-attendant and staff officer air his views more freely than before, so the whole empire will see that the sage Son of Heaven does not punish men for speaking out.

sentences.zh: 若此，則流言消釋，疑惑著明。」

translations.literal: If thus, then drifting words dissolve and melt, doubt and confusion are made clear.
translations.idiomatic: Rumor will die away and suspicion will clear.

sentences.zh: 鳳白行其策。

translations.literal: Feng reported and carried out his plan.
translations.idiomatic: Feng adopted the plan and put it into effect.

sentences.zh: 欽之補過將美，皆此類也。

translations.literal: Qin mending fault and advancing beauty were all of this kind.
translations.idiomatic: Du Qin habit of covering others missteps while advancing what was right ran in this vein.

sentences.zh: 優遊不仕，以壽終。

translations.literal: He wandered leisurely and did not take office, and by old age ended.
translations.idiomatic: He lived out his days in comfortable retirement, never again taking office.

sentences.zh: 欽子及昆弟支屬至二千石者且十人。

translations.literal: Qin sons and younger brothers and branch kin who reached two thousand dan were almost ten persons.
translations.idiomatic: Nearly ten of his sons, brothers, and cousins rose to the rank of two thousand piculs.

sentences.zh: 欽兄緩前免太常，以列侯奉朝請，成帝時乃薨，子業嗣。

translations.literal: Qin elder brother Huan formerly was dismissed from grand master of ceremonies, by column marquis attended court summons; in Emperor Cheng time then died; his son Ye inherited.
translations.idiomatic: His elder brother Du Huan had earlier been removed as grand master of ceremonies but kept his column marquis title with permission to attend court; he died under Emperor Cheng, and his son Du Ye inherited.

sentences.zh: 欽子 業

translations.literal: Qin son: Ye.
translations.idiomatic: Du Qin son: Du Ye.

sentences.zh: 業有材能，以列侯選，復為太常。

translations.literal: Ye had talent and ability; by column marquis selection, again became grand master of ceremonies.
translations.idiomatic: Du Ye was capable; as a column marquis he was selected again for the post of grand master of ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 數言得失，不事權貴，與丞相翟方進、衛尉定陵侯淳于長不平。

translations.literal: Repeatedly he spoke of gain and loss, did not serve power and nobility, was not at peace with Chancellor Zhai Fangjin and Commandant of the Guards Marquis of Dingling Chunyu Chang.
translations.idiomatic: He often criticized policy, refused to truckle to the mighty, and was on bad terms with Chancellor Zhai Fangjin and Commandant of the Guards Chunyu Chang, marquis of Dingling.

sentences.zh: 後業坐法免官，復為函谷關都尉。

translations.literal: Afterward Ye sat guilty by law and was dismissed from office, again became Hangu Pass commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Later he lost his post for a legal infraction, then was appointed commandant of Hangu Pass.

sentences.zh: 會定陵侯長有罪，當就國，長舅紅陽侯立與業書曰：「誠哀老姊垂白，隨無狀子出關，願勿復用前事相侵。」

translations.literal: It happened that Marquis of Dingling Chang had guilt and ought to go to his state; Chang maternal uncle Marquis of Hongyang Li wrote Ye a letter, saying, I truly pity the old sister hoary-haired, following her unworthy son out the pass—I hope you will not again use former affairs to encroach.
translations.idiomatic: When Chunyu Chang was ordered to his fief for crimes, his uncle Wang Li, the marquis of Hongyang, wrote to Du Ye: "My poor white-haired sister must follow her worthless son through the pass—please do not use old scores against him."

sentences.zh: 定陵侯既出關，伏罪復發，下洛陽獄。

translations.literal: When Marquis of Dingling Chang had already gone out the pass, hidden guilt again broke out; he was sent down to Luoyang prison.
translations.idiomatic: After Chunyu Chang had crossed the pass, new evidence of guilt surfaced and he was remanded to the Luoyang jail.

sentences.zh: 丞相史搜得紅陽侯書，奏業聽請，不敬，坐免就國。

translations.literal: The chancellor clerk searched and obtained Marquis of Hongyang letter, memorialized that Ye listened to a private plea—disrespect—and sat guilty, dismissed to go to his state.
translations.idiomatic: A clerk in the chancellor's office found Wang Li's letter and impeached Du Ye for honoring an improper request; Ye was stripped of rank and sent to his fief.

sentences.zh: 業又言宜為恭王立廟京師，以章孝道。

translations.literal: Ye also spoke that it was fitting to establish a temple in the capital region for Prince Gong, thereby to display filial way.
translations.idiomatic: Du Ye also memorialized that a shrine to Prince Gong should be erected in the capital to give public honor to filial devotion.

sentences.zh: 時，高昌侯董宏亦言宜尊帝母定陶王丁後為帝太后。

translations.literal: At this time Marquis of Gaochang Dong Hong also spoke that it was fitting to honor the emperor mother, the Ding empress of King of Dingtao, as empress dowager.
translations.idiomatic: About the same time Dong Hong, marquis of Gaochang, argued that Emperor Ai's birth mother, Lady Ding, consort of the king of Dingtao, should receive the title empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 大司空師丹等劾宏誤朝不道，坐免為庶人，業復上書訟宏。

translations.literal: Grand minister of works Shi Dan and others impeached Hong for misleading the court and non-dao; he sat guilty and was dismissed to be commoner; Ye again submitted a memorial suing for Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Minister of Works Shi Dan and others impeached Dong Hong for misleading the court; Hong was stripped of rank and reduced to commoner status. Du Ye then filed another memorial in his defense.

sentences.zh: 前後所言皆合指施行，朱博果見拔用。

translations.literal: What he spoke of before and after all matched intent and was carried out; Zhu Bo indeed was seen pulled up and employed.
translations.idiomatic: Memorial after memorial hit the mark and was implemented; Zhu Bo in particular owed his rise to Du Ye's backing.

sentences.zh: 業由是征，復為太常。

translations.literal: Ye from this was summoned, again became grand master of ceremonies.
translations.idiomatic: Du Ye was recalled to the capital and reappointed grand master of ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，左遷上黨都尉。

translations.literal: After more than a year, he was demoted left to be commandant of Shangdang.
translations.idiomatic: A little over a year later he was relegated to commandant of Shangdang.

sentences.zh: 會司隸奏業為太常選舉不實，業坐免官，復就國。

translations.literal: It happened that the metropolitan superintendent memorialized that Ye as grand master of ceremonies had selections and recommendations not true; Ye sat guilty, dismissed from office, again went to his state.
translations.idiomatic: Then the metropolitan superintendent charged him with false entries in the lists of candidates he had sponsored as grand master of ceremonies. Du Ye lost his post once more and returned to his fief.

sentences.zh: 哀帝崩，王莽秉政，諸前議立廟尊號者皆免，徙合浦。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai died, Wang Mang held government; those who formerly had deliberated establishing temples and honoring titles were all dismissed and moved to Hepu.
translations.idiomatic: After Emperor Ai died and Wang Mang took charge, everyone who had backed the temple and title controversy was removed from office and banished to Hepu.

sentences.zh: 業以前罷黜，故見闊略，憂恐，發病死。

translations.literal: Ye because formerly had been dismissed and demoted, therefore was treated with lenient omission; worried and afraid, he fell ill and died.
translations.idiomatic: Because Du Ye had already been disgraced earlier, Mang let him off lightly; still, Ye lived in such dread that he sickened and died.

sentences.zh: 業成帝初尚帝妹穎邑公主，主無子，薨，業家上書求還京師與主合葬，不許，而賜謚曰荒侯，傳子至孫絕。

translations.literal: Ye at Emperor Cheng beginning had married the emperor younger sister Princess Yingyi; the princess had no sons; when she died, Ye family submitted a memorial requesting to return to the capital region and be jointly buried with the princess—not permitted; yet he was granted posthumous title Marquis Dissolute, transmitted son to grandson and cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Cheng's reign Du Ye had married Princess Yingyi, the emperor's sister. She died without issue, and his family petitioned to bring his remains to the capital for burial beside hers. The court refused but bestowed the posthumous epithet Dissolute Marquis. The marquisate passed to his son and then his grandson before the line failed.

sentences.zh: 初，杜周武帝時徙茂陵，至延年徙杜陵雲。

translations.literal: At first Du Zhou in Emperor Wu time moved to Maoling; reaching Yannian he moved to Duling—it is said.
translations.idiomatic: Du Zhou's household had been relocated to Maoling under Emperor Wu; by Du Yannian's generation the family had settled at Duling—or so the note runs.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：張湯、杜周並起文墨小吏，致位三公，列於酷吏。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Zhang Tang and Du Zhou together rose from ink-and-brush petty clerks, reached position among the three dukes, were arrayed among cruel clerks.
translations.idiomatic: The historian concludes: Zhang Tang and Du Zhou both climbed from scribal obscurity to the three highest offices and earned their place among the "cruel officials."

sentences.zh: 而俱有良子，德器自過，爵位尊顯，繼世立朝，相與提衡，至於建武，杜氏爵乃獨絕，跡其福祚、元功儒林之後莫能及也。

translations.literal: Yet both had good sons whose virtue and vessel surpassed themselves; noble rank was honored and prominent, succeeding generations stood at court, mutually lifting the balance—down to Jianwu the Du clan noble rank then alone was cut off; tracing their blessing fortune—even descendants of first merit and Confucian forest could not reach them.
translations.idiomatic: Yet each raised worthy sons who surpassed him in moral stature. Generation after generation they held high office, matching the greatest houses at court—until the Jianwu restoration, when the Du marquisate alone died out. In the sheer length of their good fortune even the posterity of the founding generals and the erudite school could not equal them.

sentences.zh: 自謂唐杜苗裔，豈其然乎？

translations.literal: They called themselves Tang-Du posterity—was it indeed so?
translations.idiomatic: They claimed descent from the ancient lords of Tang and Du—who can say whether it was true?

sentences.zh: 及欽浮沉當世，好謀而成，以建始之初深陳女戒，終如其言，庶幾乎《關雎》之見微，非夫浮華博習之徒所能規也。

translations.literal: As to Qin floating and sinking in the age, loving stratagem and completing it—with the Jianshi beginning deeply displaying women warning, in the end as his words—perhaps near to Guanju seeing the subtle—not something floating-flower broad-study class could measure.
translations.idiomatic: Du Qin moved with his times, relished intrigue, and carried his plans through. At the opening of the Jianshi era he laid out the peril of the harem with frightening clarity, and events bore him out—close to the moral discernment of the "Guanju" ode, and far beyond the compass of shallow rhetoricians.

sentences.zh: 業因勢而抵垝，稱硃博，毀師丹，愛憎之議可不畏哉

translations.literal: Ye by the situation struck the crack, praised Zhu Bo, destroyed Shi Dan—love-and-hate discussion—can one not fear!
translations.idiomatic: Du Ye exploited every shift in power to drive wedges: he extolled Zhu Bo and ruined Shi Dan. When favor and spite steer memorials at court, the lesson should give us pause.
